summary: Can a meal-offering's handful (kometz) removed at night be replaced with a new handful during the day? Rabbi Zeira raises objections from a baraita about principles governing disqualified offerings. Rava analyzes the dispute. The page concludes with Temple hygiene practices: priests who needed to relieve themselves would leave the service area, and a euphemism ('covering one's feet') is used throughout.

glossary.en: Handful; the measured scoop of flour removed from a meal-offering (mincha) and burned on the altar.
Covering of legs; a biblical euphemism for relieving oneself (defecation).
Meal-offering; a grain offering, part of which (the kometz) is burned on the altar.

rashiTranslations.en: Can we restore a nighttime kometz with a new daytime handful?
Baraita principle: any offering whose time has not yet come cannot be disqualified by improper intent.
R. Zeira objects: the baraita principle shows the nighttime kometz cannot be replaced.
What should be done? Leave the offering in place until a new kometz can be taken.
Rava says: R. Zeira raises a good objection; the father of R. Avin is also raising a good point.
If nighttime service is not premature, when Shabbat comes, the wood arrangement should be left in place.
Mishna: Temple principle - anyone who 'covers his legs' (needs bathroom) leaves service.
As the master says (Menachot 44a) 'their meal and libation offerings' - even at night.
Is sanctified in a vessel at night.
If placed in them at night, it has bodily sanctity.
The baraita teaches: whatever is offered by day is sanctified during the day, but not at night.
Meaning: not sanctified at night for the purpose of being offered.
But it is sanctified to become susceptible to invalidation.
By a tevul yom or one lacking atonement who touches it.
Or if it went outside the boundary.
Or if it remained overnight - it would be invalidated by overnight.
Since we rule in Zevachim (87a) that dawn renders overnight invalid.
The night-time kometz which was pinched is invalidated overnight.
It cannot revert to being meal offering.
Invalid - since it was not arranged on Shabbat to stand there the required seven days.
It lacks time and is incomplete.
I add a note: perhaps we should not read 'invalid' here.
Since the conclusion of the passage is that arranging on the first day is not considered arrangement.
But like a monkey arranging it.
And it is as if it were placed in a box.
Why would the bread be invalidated by burning the frankincense bowls?
The table did not sanctify the bread at all.
Unless they clear it on the coming Shabbat and re-arrange it.
This is also proven by the teaching 'what shall he do? He leaves it.'
Implying there is a remedy.
R. Chananya of Rome reads: 'R. Zeira objected.'
And the frankincense bowls were burned on Shabbat: what shall he do?
Even though the bowls were burned on Shabbat as usual, bring new bowls and re-arrange.
Even after burning the bowls on Shabbat as usual, bring new bowls and arrange with the bread.
What shall he do - leave it on the table until the next Shabbat.
It should be sanctified and invalidated - since sacred vessels sanctify out of time.
The table should sanctify the bread from Sunday onward and invalidate it by nighttime arrangement.
Rava said: he who objects well objects; and the father of R. Avin.
From whom the objection was raised - he does not mean an amora's teaching was refuted.
It is a Tannaitic text and we must reconcile the texts without contradiction.
The tanna of the father of R. Avin holds: nighttime is not a deficiency of time.
Since it could be sanctified in the vessel in the morning; therefore sacred vessels sanctify the kometz at night.
But a full day is a deficiency of time - something not to be offered until tomorrow.
Lacks a full day of time.
When Friday evening arrives - nighttime of Shabbat when it can be sanctified next morning.
The table sanctifies it; and at night it is invalidated for overnight.
He removed it early on Friday - before Shabbat night.
Not as commanded - lacking the proper time.
It is as if a monkey arranged it - without intent, as if placed in a box.
Rashi commentary on line 50 of 29b.
Rashi commentary on line 51 of 29b.
Rashi commentary on line 52 of 29b.
Rashi commentary on line 53 of 29b.
Rashi commentary on line 54 of 29b.

display.title: On hold, not invalid: replacing a nighttime kometz
display.whats: Can the nighttime kometz of a meal-offering be replaced by a new daytime handful? Rabbi Zeira objects from baraita principles. Rava acknowledges both sides have valid points. The page concludes with Temple hygiene protocol.
display.hint: The nighttime kometz question hinges on whether a 'disqualified' offering that can theoretically become valid (once day breaks) is in the same category as an offering whose time has permanently passed.

learning.learnerQuestion: If a kometz is removed at night, when offerings cannot be burned, is it permanently disqualified, or can a fresh handful simply be taken once day breaks?
learning.coreTension: The father of Rabbi Avin raises the question of whether a nighttime kometz can be replaced by a new daytime handful. Rabbi Zeira objects from a baraita: an offering whose proper time has not yet arrived cannot be disqualified by improper intent - implying that a nighttime kometz occupies a protected, intermediate status rather than being simply discardable and replaceable at will.
learning.coreMove: Rava resolves the tension not by picking a side but by affirming both: Rabbi Zeira's objection is well-founded and the father of Rabbi Avin's principle is also well-founded, each describing a different layer of the same status. The practical instruction follows from this: leave the kometz in place without burning it until day breaks, then take a new kometz at the proper time. The page closes with the Mishna's related Temple protocol - any priest who needs to relieve himself must step away from the service area first.
learning.resolution: The practical ruling is to leave the kometz in place overnight without burning it, and take a new kometz once day breaks - the nighttime kometz is treated as suspended rather than disqualified.

takeaway.text: An offering whose proper time simply hasn't arrived yet is not the same halachic category as one that is actually disqualified - the nighttime kometz waits for its moment rather than being thrown out.

learning.ahaMoment: The nighttime kometz is neither 'valid' nor 'permanently invalid' - it is in a waiting state, and this temporal ambiguity drives the entire halachic analysis.
learning.learningBlocker: The sugya does not say whether the second, daytime kometz is a continuation of the same offering or counts as an entirely new act of removal - it settles the practical outcome (wait, then take a fresh handful) without addressing that conceptual question.
learning.memoryAnchor: Nighttime kometz = on hold. Wait for day, then take a fresh handful. Don't discard - but don't burn at night either.

argumentFlow.label: Can nighttime kometz be replaced?
argumentFlow.text: Can a kometz removed at night (when altar service is invalid) be replaced with a fresh handful once day breaks?

argumentFlow.label: Baraita: offerings not-yet-ready cannot be disqualified
argumentFlow.text: A baraita teaches: an offering whose proper time has not yet arrived cannot be disqualified by improper intent - suggesting nighttime kometz is in a protected state.

argumentFlow.label: R. Zeira: this proves no replacement
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Zeira: the baraita principle shows a nighttime kometz cannot simply be replaced - it has a valid future and must wait for its time.

argumentFlow.label: Leave in place, take new kometz at day
argumentFlow.text: Practical ruling: leave the offering in place overnight; in the morning take a new kometz properly.

argumentFlow.label: Rava: both sides valid
argumentFlow.text: Rava says both Rabbi Zeira's objection and the father of Rabbi Avin's principle are correctly stated - they resolve different aspects of the question.

argumentFlow.label: Mishna: bathroom = leave service
argumentFlow.text: Any priest who needs to relieve himself ('cover his legs') must leave the service area before doing so.

misconceptions.misconception: A kometz removed at night is permanently invalid and must be discarded.
misconceptions.correction: It is actually in a waiting state and can be followed by a valid daytime kometz.

misconceptions.misconception: The baraita about 'offerings not yet ready' applies only to future-timed offerings.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara applies it to nighttime offerings that will become valid at dawn.

visualizableElements.label: Nighttime kometz status
visualizableElements.description: Kometz removed at night: in waiting state. Dawn breaks: valid time resumes. New kometz taken in daytime.

visualizableElements.label: Temple service area exit for hygiene
visualizableElements.description: Priests step outside the service area to relieve themselves before returning to Temple duty

quizSeeds.question: What is the ruling if a priest takes the kometz from a meal-offering at night?
quizSeeds.answer: The offering is left in place; the priest waits until daytime and takes a new, valid kometz then.

quizSeeds.question: What does the Temple protocol require of a priest who needs to relieve himself?
quizSeeds.answer: The priest must leave the Temple service area before relieving himself ('covering his legs').

sugyot.finalRuling: A nighttime kometz is not permanently disqualified - a new daytime kometz may be taken. Priests must leave service to relieve themselves.
